result:
The rain doesn’t fall in this city; it hangs. A perpetual silver mist that clings to coats, eyelashes, and half-remembered regrets. I like it here. The streets feel like they’re keeping secrets, and the buildings lean toward one another like old friends who’ve run out of new things to say but still enjoy the company.

Yesterday I watched a woman feed pigeons with what looked like torn-up love letters. She didn’t cry. She just let the birds carry away tiny pieces of someone else’s story. I almost asked her whose words she was scattering, but some moments are more beautiful when they remain untranslated.

There’s a jazz club downstairs that never closes. The trumpet player has been 67 for the last fourteen years. He claims the music keeps him from aging. I believe him. Some nights I fall asleep to the sound of his horn drifting through the floorboards like warm smoke, and I dream in minor keys.

I’ve decided that growing older shouldn’t feel like a defeat. It should feel like editing a very long, occasionally embarrassing manuscript. You cut the parts that no longer serve the story. You keep the strange, tender sentences that somehow still work. You learn which memories are load-bearing and which ones are just decorative.

The kettle is screaming. The cat is judging me from the windowsill. Outside, the rain continues its patient, endless work of polishing the world one invisible layer at a time.

I think I’ll make tea and misbehave gently for the rest of the afternoon. There’s a novel that needs ignoring and a saxophone that needs listening to. The day feels like it might forgive me for being exactly who I am.

(Word count: 278)
